are an English [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") band from [Camden Town](/wiki/Camden_Town "Camden Town"), north west London, who formed in 1976. One of the most prominent bands of the late-1970s/early-1980s [two-tone](/wiki/Two-tone_(music_genre) "Two-tone (music genre)") ska revival, they continue to perform with six of the seven members of their original line-up.[[1]](#cite_note-allmusic-1)[[a]](#cite_note-2) Madness's most successful period was from 1980 to 1986, when the band's songs spent a total of 214 weeks on the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart"), jointly holding the record with English [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") group [UB40](/wiki/UB40 "UB40") for most weeks spent by a group in the UK singles chart during the 1980s.[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-suggsimdb-4)[[4]](#cite_note-The_News-5)

**Madness** are an English [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") band from [Camden Town](/wiki/Camden_Town "Camden Town"), north west London, who formed in 1976. One of the most prominent bands of the late-1970s/early-1980s [two-tone](/wiki/Two-tone_(music_genre) "Two-tone (music genre)") ska revival, they continue to perform with six of the seven members of their original line-up.[[1]](#cite_note-allmusic-1)[[a]](#cite_note-2) Madness's most successful period was from 1980 to 1986, when the band's songs spent a total of 214 weeks on the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart"), jointly holding the record with English [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") group [UB40](/wiki/UB40 "UB40") for most weeks spent by a group in the UK singles chart during the 1980s.[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-suggsimdb-4)[[4]](#cite_note-The_News-5)

Madness have had 16 singles reach the UK top ten, including "[One Step Beyond](/wiki/One_Step_Beyond_(song) "One Step Beyond (song)")", "[Baggy Trousers](/wiki/Baggy_Trousers "Baggy Trousers")" and "[It Must Be Love](/wiki/It_Must_Be_Love_(Labi_Siffre_song)#Madness_version "It Must Be Love (Labi Siffre song)")"; one UK number-one single, "[House of Fun](/wiki/House_of_Fun "House of Fun")"; and two number ones in Ireland, "House of Fun" and "[Wings of a Dove](/wiki/Wings_of_a_Dove_(Madness_song) "Wings of a Dove (Madness song)")". "[Our House](/wiki/Our_House_(Madness_song) "Our House (Madness song)")" was their biggest US hit, reaching number 7 on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100"). In 2000, the band received the [Ivor Novello Award](/wiki/Ivor_Novello_Award "Ivor Novello Award") from the [British Academy of Songwriters, Composers and Authors](/wiki/British_Academy_of_Songwriters,_Composers_and_Authors "British Academy of Songwriters, Composers and Authors") for Outstanding Song Collection.[[5]](#cite_note-novellobbc-6)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1976–1978: Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1976–1978: Formation")]

The core of the band formed as the North London Invaders in 1976, and included [Mike Barson](/wiki/Mike_Barson "Mike Barson") (Monsieur Barso) on keyboards, [Chris Foreman](/wiki/Chris_Foreman "Chris Foreman") (Chrissy Boy) on guitar and [Lee Thompson](/wiki/Lee_Thompson_(saxophonist) "Lee Thompson (saxophonist)") (Kix) on saxophone.[[6]](#cite_note-madness76-7) They later recruited John Hasler on drums and [Cathal Smyth](/wiki/Chas_Smash "Chas Smash") (better known as [Chas Smash](/wiki/Chas_Smash "Chas Smash")) on bass guitar. Later in the year, they were joined by lead vocalist [Dikran Tulaine](/wiki/Dikran_Tulaine "Dikran Tulaine").[[6]](#cite_note-madness76-7)

This six-piece line-up lasted until partway through 1977, when Graham McPherson (better known as [Suggs](/wiki/Suggs "Suggs")) took over the lead vocals after seeing the band perform in a friend's garden.[[7]](#cite_note-madness77-8) Tulaine went on to be an actor.[[8]](#cite_note-DT1-9) Smyth, who left after an argument with Barson, was replaced with Gavin Rodgers, Barson's girlfriend's brother.[[7]](#cite_note-madness77-8) McPherson was kicked out of the band by Barson for choosing too often to watch [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_FC "Chelsea FC") instead of rehearsing.[[9]](#cite_note-madness78-10) Thompson left the band after Barson criticised his saxophone playing.[[9]](#cite_note-madness78-10)

By 1978, the band had allowed McPherson to return as a vocalist after he had filled in temporarily for Hasler (who had taken over vocals when McPherson was removed).[[9]](#cite_note-madness78-10) Thompson returned after patching things up with Barson. Drummer [Dan Woodgate](/wiki/Dan_Woodgate "Dan Woodgate") (Woody) and bassist [Mark Bedford](/wiki/Mark_Bedford "Mark Bedford") (Bedders) also joined the band, replacing Garry Dovey and Rodgers, respectively, the latter of whom left due to his lack of faith in the band and their organisation.[[9]](#cite_note-madness78-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) After briefly changing their name to Morris and the Minors,[[11]](#cite_note-12) the band renamed itself as Madness in 1979, paying homage to one of their favourite songs by [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and reggae artist [Prince Buster](/wiki/Prince_Buster "Prince Buster").[[12]](#cite_note-madness79-13) The band remained a sextet until late 1979 when Chas Smash rejoined and officially became the seventh member of Madness as a backing vocalist and dancer.[[12]](#cite_note-madness79-13)

### 1979–1981: Early success

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1979–1981: Early success")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Dublin_Castle_94_Parkway_Camden_London_NW1_7AN.jpg/250px-Dublin_Castle_94_Parkway_Camden_London_NW1_7AN.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dublin_Castle_94_Parkway_Camden_London_NW1_7AN.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Madness_%28PRS%29_Dublin_Castle_94_Parkway_Camden_London_NW1_7AN.jpg/250px-Madness_%28PRS%29_Dublin_Castle_94_Parkway_Camden_London_NW1_7AN.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madness_(PRS)_Dublin_Castle_94_Parkway_Camden_London_NW1_7AN.jpg)

[Dublin Castle](/wiki/Dublin_Castle,_Camden "Dublin Castle, Camden") pub at 94 [Parkway](/wiki/Parkway,_Camden "Parkway, Camden"), [Camden Town](/wiki/Camden_Town "Camden Town"), London, with a close-up of the commemorative [PRS](/wiki/PRS_for_Music "PRS for Music") plaque on the wall

During 1979, the band began to attract a live following in London, being regulars at the [Dublin Castle](/wiki/Dublin_Castle,_Camden "Dublin Castle, Camden") in Camden Town.[[13]](#cite_note-14) The band's first commercial recording was the [Lee Thompson](/wiki/Lee_Thompson_(saxophonist) "Lee Thompson (saxophonist)") composition "[The Prince](/wiki/The_Prince_(song) "The Prince (song)")".[[12]](#cite_note-madness79-13) The song, like the band's name, paid homage to their idol, [Prince Buster](/wiki/Prince_Buster "Prince Buster"). The song was released through [2 Tone Records](/wiki/2_Tone_Records "2 Tone Records"), the label of [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials") founder and keyboardist [Jerry Dammers](/wiki/Jerry_Dammers "Jerry Dammers").[[12]](#cite_note-madness79-13) The song was a surprise hit, peaking in the UK music charts at number 16.[[12]](#cite_note-madness79-13) Madness toured with fellow [2 Tone](/wiki/2_Tone_(music_genre) "2 Tone (music genre)") bands the Specials and [the Selecter](/wiki/The_Selecter "The Selecter"), before recording their debut studio album.

That debut studio album, *[One Step Beyond...](/wiki/One_Step_Beyond... "One Step Beyond...")* was released by [Stiff Records](/wiki/Stiff_Records "Stiff Records").[[12]](#cite_note-madness79-13) The album included a re-recording of "The Prince" and its B-side "Madness", and the band's second and third singles: "[One Step Beyond](/wiki/One_Step_Beyond_(song) "One Step Beyond (song)")" and "[My Girl](/wiki/My_Girl_(Madness_song) "My Girl (Madness song)")". The title song was a cover of the [B-side](/wiki/B-side "B-side") of the 1960s Prince Buster hit "Al Capone". The *One Step Beyond...* album stayed in the British charts for 78 weeks, peaking at number 2.[[12]](#cite_note-madness79-13)[[14]](#cite_note-osbchart-15) Smyth performed on the album but was not an official member of the band at the time of the album's recording or release. He would formally join Madness a few weeks after *One Step Beyond...* was issued in October 1979.

After the release of "My Girl", the band felt that they had exhausted the material from *One Step Beyond...*, and did not want to release any more singles from the album.[[15]](#cite_note-madness80-16) However, [Dave Robinson](/wiki/Dave_Robinson_(music_executive) "Dave Robinson (music executive)"), head of Stiff Records, disagreed.[[15]](#cite_note-madness80-16) Eventually, a compromise was made, and the band decided to release an EP featuring one album track and three new tracks.[[15]](#cite_note-madness80-16) The result was the *[Work Rest and Play](/wiki/Work_Rest_and_Play "Work Rest and Play")* EP, which was headlined by the song "[Night Boat to Cairo](/wiki/Night_Boat_to_Cairo "Night Boat to Cairo")", from the *One Step Beyond* album.[[15]](#cite_note-madness80-16) The EP reached number 6 in the UK Singles Chart.[[15]](#cite_note-madness80-16)

In 1980, the band's second studio album, *[Absolutely](/wiki/Absolutely_(Madness_album) "Absolutely (Madness album)")* reached number 2 in the [UK Albums Chart](/wiki/UK_Albums_Chart "UK Albums Chart").[[15]](#cite_note-madness80-16) *Absolutely* spawned some of the band's biggest hits, most notably "[Baggy Trousers](/wiki/Baggy_Trousers "Baggy Trousers")", which peaked at number 3 in the UK Singles Chart.[[15]](#cite_note-madness80-16) "[Embarrassment](/wiki/Embarrassment_(song) "Embarrassment (song)")" reached number 4 in the charts, and the instrumental song "[The Return of the Los Palmas 7](/wiki/The_Return_of_the_Los_Palmas_7 "The Return of the Los Palmas 7")" climbed to number 7.[[15]](#cite_note-madness80-16) Although the album reviews were generally less enthusiastic than those of *One Step Beyond...*, they were mostly positive. [Robert Christgau](/wiki/Robert_Christgau "Robert Christgau") gave the album a favourable B− grade,[[16]](#cite_note-abrc-17) but *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* awarded the album just one out of five stars.[[17]](#cite_note-abrs-18) *Rolling Stone* was particularly scathing of the [ska revival](/wiki/Ska_revival "Ska revival") in general, stating that "*[The Specials](/wiki/The_Specials_(album) "The Specials (album)")* wasn't very good" and Madness were simply "[the Blues Brothers](/wiki/The_Blues_Brothers "The Blues Brothers") with English accents".[[17]](#cite_note-abrs-18)

A drama-documentary film titled *[Take It or Leave It](/wiki/Take_It_or_Leave_It_(1981_film) "Take It or Leave It (1981 film)")* was released in 1981, featuring the band members playing themselves in a re-creation of their early days to the then-current period.[[18]](#cite_note-theargus1-19) Live recordings of Madness performances as well as those by other 2 Tone bands were used in the 1981 documentary film and soundtrack album *[Dance Craze](/wiki/Dance_Craze "Dance Craze")*.

### 1981–1983: Change of direction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1981–1983: Change of direction")]

In 1981, the band's third studio album, *[7](/wiki/7_(Madness_album) "7 (Madness album)")*, reached number 5 in the UK Albums Chart and contained three hit singles: "[Grey Day](/wiki/Grey_Day "Grey Day")" (no. 4, April 1981), "[Shut Up](/wiki/Shut_Up_(Madness_song) "Shut Up (Madness song)")" (no. 7, September 1981), and "[Cardiac Arrest](/wiki/Cardiac_Arrest_(Madness_song) "Cardiac Arrest (Madness song)")" (no. 14, February 1982).[[19]](#cite_note-madness81-20) In an article in 1979, [Chris Foreman](/wiki/Chris_Foreman "Chris Foreman") explained that the band's music would move with the times, and change styles as time goes on.[[20]](#cite_note-soundsmag-21) This was shown to be the case, as unlike the two ska-filled, fast-paced albums that preceded it, *7* was something of a change in direction. [Suggs](/wiki/Suggs "Suggs")' vocal performance changed significantly, and his strong accent from the previous studio albums had been watered down.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The album strayed from the ska-influenced sound of *One Step Beyond...* and *Absolutely* and moved towards a pop sound; a trend that continued with subsequent studio albums.

Near the end of 1981, Madness released one of their most recognised songs: a cover of [Labi Siffre](/wiki/Labi_Siffre "Labi Siffre")'s 1971 hit "[It Must Be Love](/wiki/It_Must_Be_Love_(Labi_Siffre_song)#Madness_version "It Must Be Love (Labi Siffre song)")".[[19]](#cite_note-madness81-20) The song climbed to number 4 in the UK, and in 1983, the song peaked at number 33 in the US charts.[[19]](#cite_note-madness81-20)[[21]](#cite_note-2tone1-22) In 1982, Madness released their only number 1 hit to date, "[House of Fun](/wiki/House_of_Fun "House of Fun")"[[22]](#cite_note-Sony/ATV-23) and also reached number 1 in the album charts with their first compilation, *[Complete Madness](/wiki/Complete_Madness "Complete Madness")* (1982). In this period, the band starred in a series of commercials in Japan for the [Honda City](/wiki/Honda_City_(AA) "Honda City (AA)") hatchback and [Honda Motocompo](/wiki/Honda_Motocompo "Honda Motocompo") scooter, in which they sang the original composition "In the City" and a modified version of their 1982 single "[Driving in My Car](/wiki/Driving_in_My_Car "Driving in My Car")"; a full version of "In the City" was released on *Complete Madness*.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

In November 1982, they released their fourth studio album, *[The Rise & Fall](/wiki/The_Rise_%26_Fall "The Rise & Fall")*, which was well received in the UK, but did not get an American release. Instead, many of its songs were included on the US compilation *[Madness](/wiki/Madness_(Madness_album) "Madness (Madness album)")* (1983), including "[Our House](/wiki/Our_House_(Madness_song) "Our House (Madness song)")", which was their most internationally successful single to date. "Our House" reached number 5 in the UK music charts and number 7 in the US charts; it was also performed on *The Young Ones*.[[24]](#cite_note-madnessstory5-25) Many reviewers compared *The Rise & Fall* to [the Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks")' *[The Village Green Preservation Society](/wiki/The_Kinks_Are_the_Village_Green_Preservation_Society "The Kinks Are the Village Green Preservation Society")* (1968),[[25]](#cite_note-amriseandfall-26) and it is at times retrospectively considered a [concept album](/wiki/Concept_album "Concept album"). The album also featured "Primrose Hill", which was more similar to [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") song "[Strawberry Fields Forever](/wiki/Strawberry_Fields_Forever "Strawberry Fields Forever")", containing similar psychedelic imagery and a layered arrangement.[[26]](#cite_note-amprimrose-27)

### 1983–1986: Decline and break-up

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1983–1986: Decline and break-up")]

In 1983, their single "[Wings of a Dove](/wiki/Wings_of_a_Dove_(Madness_song) "Wings of a Dove (Madness song)")" peaked at number 2 in the UK charts, followed by "[The Sun and the Rain](/wiki/The_Sun_and_the_Rain "The Sun and the Rain")" (no. 5, November 1983).[[27]](#cite_note-madness83-28) Their following studio album, *[Keep Moving](/wiki/Keep_Moving_(Madness_album) "Keep Moving (Madness album)")*, peaked at number 6 in the UK Albums Chart, and two singles from that album reached the top 20 in the UK Singles Chart.[[28]](#cite_note-madness84-29) The album received some good reviews, with *Rolling Stone* magazine giving the album four out of five stars, applauding the band's changing sound.[[29]](#cite_note-rskm-30) This was an improvement as the last album reviewed by the magazine, *Absolutely*, was heavily criticised.[[17]](#cite_note-abrs-18)

#### Mike Barson's Departure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Mike Barson's Departure")]

> "Mike Barson has decided to retire from Madness and the wonderful world of
> pop music. The mild mannered foundation stone will be sorely missed
> by Madness with sadness."

—"Barson Cops Out", Press Statement for *[Record Mirror](/wiki/Record_Mirror "Record Mirror")*, January 14 1984.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

As the musical lynchpin of the band, Barson had become increasingly exhausted by the pressure of being in the band by this time. This sentiment started in late 1982 during the production of *The Rise and Fall*, during which Barson moved to Amsterdam with his wife.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) Barson also became very reclusive from the band's public image and started to cover his face in photographs. Additionally, while rehearsing *Keep Moving*, Barson disappeared on a holiday throughout Europe in a camper van with his wife and dog, holding up the release of "Wings Of A Dove" and fueling rumours that he was planning on leaving the band.[[33]](#cite_note-:1-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35)

On October 4th of 1983, the band were rehearsing and discussing a possible television series, which was being written for them by [Ben Elton](/wiki/Ben_Elton "Ben Elton") and [Richard Curtis](/wiki/Richard_Curtis "Richard Curtis"). Barson then informed the band that he would not be able to take part because he intended to leave the band, as he was tired of the music business and wanted to spend more time with his wife. Barson agreed to finish recording the album *Keep Moving;* he left after playing for the last time with the band at the [Lyceum Ballroom](/wiki/Lyceum_Ballroom "Lyceum Ballroom") on 21 December 1983.

James Mackie[[35]](#cite_note-36)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] took Barson's place, appearing with Madness on the US television show *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")* on 14 April 1984. After leaving the band, Barson returned to the UK for the filming of two music videos as he had played on the tracks, "[Michael Caine](/wiki/Michael_Caine_(song) "Michael Caine (song)")" and "[One Better Day](/wiki/One_Better_Day "One Better Day")". Barson finished live performance with the band in 1983, and officially left the band in June 1984, following the release of "One Better Day".[[28]](#cite_note-madness84-29) [Paul Carrack](/wiki/Paul_Carrack "Paul Carrack") took Barson's place whilst the band toured America in early 1984. The six remaining members left [Stiff Records](/wiki/Stiff_Records "Stiff Records") and formed their own label, [Zarjazz Records](/wiki/Zarjazz_Records "Zarjazz Records"), which was a sub-label of [Virgin Records](/wiki/Virgin_Records "Virgin Records").[[28]](#cite_note-madness84-29)

#### Post-Stiff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Post-Stiff")]

In 1985, the label released the band's sixth studio album, *[Mad Not Mad](/wiki/Mad_Not_Mad "Mad Not Mad")*. Barson's usual keyboard parts were filled by an emphasis on synthesisers provided by [Steve Nieve](/wiki/Steve_Nieve "Steve Nieve") of [the Attractions](/wiki/The_Attractions "The Attractions"). In later years, frontman [Suggs](/wiki/Suggs "Suggs") has described the album as a "polished turd".[[36]](#cite_note-storychap6-37) The album reached number 16 in the UK charts, which is the band's lowest position on the album charts to date. Despite the poor chart showing, the album was listed as number 55 in *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")*'s "All Time 100 Albums".[[37]](#cite_note-nmelist-38) The singles for the album fared even worse, with "[Yesterday's Men](/wiki/Yesterday%27s_Men "Yesterday's Men")" peaking at number 18 in the UK charts. The subsequent singles, "[Uncle Sam](/wiki/Uncle_Sam_(song) "Uncle Sam (song)")" and "[Sweetest Girl](/wiki/Sweetest_Girl_(Madness_song) "Sweetest Girl (Madness song)")", failed to make the top 20, which was a first for Madness singles.

The band then attempted to record a new studio album, and 11 [demo](/wiki/Demo_(music) "Demo (music)") tracks were recorded.[[36]](#cite_note-storychap6-37) However, musical differences arose between the band members. The [untitled album](/wiki/Unreleased_Madness_album "Unreleased Madness album") went unreleased, and in September 1986, the band announced that they were to break up.[[36]](#cite_note-storychap6-37)[[38]](#cite_note-madness86-39) Barson rejoined the band for a farewell single, "[(Waiting For) The Ghost Train](/wiki/(Waiting_For)_The_Ghost_Train "(Waiting For) The Ghost Train")", but did not appear in the music video.[[38]](#cite_note-madness86-39) Following the release of the single,[[22]](#cite_note-Sony/ATV-23) which reached a high of number 18 in the UK, and the release of their second compilation album, *[Utter Madness](/wiki/Utter_Madness "Utter Madness")*, which reached a high of number 29, the band broke up.

### 1987–1991: Interim period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1987–1991: Interim period")]

Soon after the split, four members of the band – Suggs, Carl Smyth, Lee Thompson and Chris Foreman – regrouped under the name, The Madness, having tried several different bandnames, such as [The Wasp Factory](/wiki/The_Wasp_Factory "The Wasp Factory"), Earthmen, and More. They then spent a year recording and producing their debut album at Liquidator Studios, which Madness still owned.[[39]](#cite_note-40) In April 1988, they released their eponymous debut album, *[The Madness](/wiki/The_Madness_(The_Madness_album) "The Madness (The Madness album)")*, and released two singles, "[I Pronounce You](/wiki/I_Pronounce_You "I Pronounce You")", and "[What's That](/wiki/What%27s_That "What's That")". Despite initial optimism about their future, their lacklustre commercial performances, with the album only charting at No. 65 in the UK for one week and neither of the singles reaching the top 40, caused the band to split by the end of the year.[[40]](#cite_note-41) Retrospectively, the band members expressed extreme dissatisfaction with the project, with Lee Thompson referring to it as their "Shitting Bricks" period" in the album's liner notes.[[41]](#cite_note-42) Woodgate was particularly upset by the project, thinking of it as "a complete cop out" at the time.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

> "After The Madness, Suggs, Cathal, Lee and I kind of scattered. Lee and I formed a band, The Nutty Boys, and went off on all sorts of adventures, and released and album, Crunch!. Suggs had a solo career. Cathal was working at Go! Discs as an A&R man - he used to come and see Lee and I, but he never signed us up! Bedders and Woody were in Voice Of The Beehive. I never in my wildest dreams thought that Madness would reform."

—Chris Foreman, *Wonderful* Liner Notes[[43]](#cite_note-44)

Meanwhile, [Woodgate](/wiki/Dan_Woodgate "Dan Woodgate") found success as the drummer for the band [Voice of the Beehive](/wiki/Voice_of_the_Beehive "Voice of the Beehive"), who had several UK hit singles such as "[Don't Call Me Baby](/wiki/Don%27t_Call_Me_Baby_(Voice_of_the_Beehive_song) "Don't Call Me Baby (Voice of the Beehive song)")", "I Say Nothing", and "[Monsters and Angels](/wiki/Monsters_and_Angels "Monsters and Angels")". [Mark Bedford](/wiki/Mark_Bedford "Mark Bedford") also joined the band alongside Woodgate in its formative stages, but left to pursue study graphic design at the [London College of Printing](/wiki/London_College_of_Communication "London College of Communication").[[33]](#cite_note-:1-34) He remained partially active in the music business, later collaborating with [Terry Edwards](/wiki/Terry_Edwards "Terry Edwards") to form the jazz outfit BUtterfield 8 in 1988, as well as playing bass guitar on [Morrissey](/wiki/Morrissey "Morrissey")'s second solo album *[Kill Uncle](/wiki/Kill_Uncle "Kill Uncle"),* released in 1991. [Thompson](/wiki/Lee_Thompson_(saxophonist) "Lee Thompson (saxophonist)") and [Foreman](/wiki/Chris_Foreman "Chris Foreman") formed a ska band called [The Nutty Boys](#The_Nutty_Boys_(Crunch!)) and released an album *[Crunch!](/wiki/Crunch!_(The_Nutty_Boys_album) "Crunch! (The Nutty Boys album)")* in 1990. Along with the album having modest critical and commercial success, they regularly played the live circuit in London and toured throughout Europe. Some members of Madness, such as Suggs, Carl, Barson, and Bedford, attended their early gigs between 1990 and 1991.[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46)

[Carl](/wiki/Chas_Smash "Chas Smash") became more involved in the industry side of the music business, becoming an A&R executive for [Go! Discs](/wiki/Go!_Discs "Go! Discs") in 1990. Throughout his tenure, he signed [The Stairs](/wiki/The_Stairs "The Stairs"), and worked with [The Beautiful South](/wiki/The_Beautiful_South "The Beautiful South"), [Paul Weller](/wiki/Paul_Weller "Paul Weller"), and [The La's](/wiki/The_La%27s "The La's"). He also developed a close friendship with Morrissey at that time and introduced [Boz Boorer](/wiki/Boz_Boorer "Boz Boorer") to him, who continued to work with Morrissey for the next 3 decades. Morrissey even asked Carl to manage him, but he declined the offer. He instead provided backing vocals on Morrissey's cover of [The Jam's](/wiki/The_Jam "The Jam") song "That's Entertainment", which was a B-side for the single "[Sing Your Life](/wiki/Sing_Your_Life "Sing Your Life")" (1991).

However, [Suggs](/wiki/Suggs "Suggs") had difficulty figuring out his life as he initially struggled with paranoia and a breakdown after the split. He was very worried about how he would provide for his family, which caused him to start taking ecstasy and seeing a therapist.[[46]](#cite_note-47) Eventually, Suggs started pursuing various endeavours, including acting in *The Press Gang,* the 1990 indie film *The Final Frame*, and the 1989 film *[The Tall Guy](/wiki/The_Tall_Guy "The Tall Guy")*. He also tried stand-up comedy for a short period in the summer of 1989, but he was unsuccessful in that field.[[47]](#cite_note-48) He also became a TV presenter on the [BSB Power Station](/wiki/The_Power_Station_(TV_channel) "The Power Station (TV channel)"), hosting his own show *Suggs On Saturday* from 1990 to 1991. Suggs then became the manager and producer for [The Farm](/wiki/The_Farm_(British_band) "The Farm (British band)") from 1990 to 1992, producing their 1991 No. 1 album *[Spartacus](/wiki/Spartacus_(The_Farm_album) "Spartacus (The Farm album)")*, as well their Top 10 UK Hit Singles, "[Groovy Train](/wiki/Groovy_Train "Groovy Train")" (1990) and "[All Together Now](/wiki/All_Together_Now_(The_Farm_song) "All Together Now (The Farm song)")" (1990). Suggs also collaborated with Morrissey, providing backing vocals on some of his singles, such as "[Picadilly Palare](/wiki/Piccadilly_Palare "Piccadilly Palare")" (1990) and "Sing Your Life" (1991).

Throughout this period, the members of the band still stayed in touch and worked together. Thompson, Bedford, and Foreman started playing together in Terry Edward's All Stars.[[48]](#cite_note-:0-49) [Mike Barson](/wiki/Mike_Barson "Mike Barson"), who was still retired in Amsterdam, regularly wrote and produced songs with some of his former bandmates, namely Lee Thompson and Suggs, who came to visit him, many of which appeared on subsequent Madness albums and on Suggs' 1995 solo debut album *[The Lone Ranger](/wiki/The_Lone_Ranger_(album) "The Lone Ranger (album)")*.[[48]](#cite_note-:0-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) Carl also regularly visited Lee and Chris at Liquidator Studios when they were working on *Crunch!.* Following the release of the compilation albums *[It's... Madness](/wiki/It%27s..._Madness "It's... Madness")* (1990) and *[It's... Madness Too](/wiki/It%27s..._Madness_Too "It's... Madness Too")* (1991), which both found moderate commercial success, the discussion of the potential release of a greatest hits album emerged, and the entire band started keeping in contact with each other regularly again through Carl, who spawned the idea of promoting the greatest hits album and ultimately initiated their comeback.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

### 1992–2003: Reunion and *Our House* musical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1992–2003: Reunion and Our House musical")]

In February 1992, "It Must Be Love" was re-released and eventually reached number 6 in the UK Singles Chart in March.[[51]](#cite_note-imblchart-52) Following that, the singles compilation album *[Divine Madness](/wiki/Divine_Madness_(Madness_album) "Divine Madness (Madness album)")* (1992) was released and peaked at number 1 in the album charts.[[52]](#cite_note-53) Madness then announced plans for a reunion concert, [Madstock!](/wiki/Madstock! "Madstock!"), which was held at [Finsbury Park](/wiki/Finsbury_Park "Finsbury Park"), London on 8 and 9 August of that year.[[53]](#cite_note-totp-54) The original line-up reunited, performing together for the first time since Barson left the band in 1984. Over 75,000 fans attended the weekend festival, and the dancing of the crowd caused some nearby tower blocks to shake perceptibly as they [resonated](/wiki/Resonance "Resonance") with the frequency of the music.[[3]](#cite_note-suggsimdb-4)[[54]](#cite_note-quake3-55)[[55]](#cite_note-quake2-56)[[56]](#cite_note-quake-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58)

Morrissey's set as a supporting act was met with negative reception and heckling by the audience, who threw various things at the stage, causing him and his band to walk off after only nine songs.[[58]](#cite_note-59) The band members had various reactions, with [Barson](/wiki/Mike_Barson "Mike Barson") retroactively stating "The funny thing was that I had been totally cut off from the British music industry, living in Holland. [The Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths") had passed me by. I didn't know who Morrissey was."[[59]](#cite_note-60)

After the Finsbury Park comeback, a live album was released, and the associated single, "[The Harder They Come](/wiki/The_Harder_They_Come_(song)#Madness_version "The Harder They Come (song)")" (a cover of [Jimmy Cliff](/wiki/Jimmy_Cliff "Jimmy Cliff")'s 1973 song) reached number 44 in the UK, with the album reaching number 22.[[60]](#cite_note-hardertheycome-61)

The band continued to reunite for annual UK Christmas season tours and held three more Madstock! festivals; in 1994, 1996 and 1998. Also in 1998, Madness returned to America for their first tour there since 1984. The live album *[Universal Madness](/wiki/Universal_Madness "Universal Madness")* was recorded at the [Universal Amphitheatre](/wiki/Universal_Amphitheatre "Universal Amphitheatre") in L.A. and released the following year. In 1999, Madness released their first studio album since 1986, entitled *[Wonderful](/wiki/Wonderful_(Madness_album) "Wonderful (Madness album)")*. The album reached number 17 in the UK Albums Chart, and the lead single, "[Lovestruck](/wiki/Lovestruck_(Madness_song) "Lovestruck (Madness song)")", gave the band their first new top 10 hit in the UK since 1983.[[61]](#cite_note-lovestruckchart-62) Neither of the two subsequent singles from the album, "[Johnny the Horse](/wiki/Johnny_the_Horse "Johnny the Horse")" and "[Drip Fed Fred](/wiki/Drip_Fed_Fred "Drip Fed Fred")" (featuring [Ian Dury](/wiki/Ian_Dury "Ian Dury") on vocals), entered the top 40 of the UK chart.[[62]](#cite_note-jthchart-63)[[63]](#cite_note-ddfchart-64)

From 28 October 2002 to 16 August 2003, a musical based on Madness songs, *[Our House](/wiki/Our_House_(musical) "Our House (musical)")*, ran at the [Cambridge Theatre](/wiki/Cambridge_Theatre "Cambridge Theatre") in London. Madness played a role in the executive production of the show, and Suggs played a role in the production for a period, playing the central character's father.[[64]](#cite_note-ourhousebbc-65) It won an [Olivier Award](/wiki/Olivier_Award "Olivier Award") for best new musical of 2003,[[65]](#cite_note-66) and the performance was released on DVD on 1 November 2004.[[66]](#cite_note-67) There was also a previous musical based on Madness songs, *One Step Beyond!*, written by Alan Gilbey.[[67]](#cite_note-onestepbeyond93-68)[[68]](#cite_note-gilbyOSB-69) The musical had a brief run at the [Theatre Royal Stratford East](/wiki/Theatre_Royal_Stratford_East "Theatre Royal Stratford East") in 1993[[67]](#cite_note-onestepbeyond93-68)[[68]](#cite_note-gilbyOSB-69) and a run at [Putney Arts Theatre](/wiki/Putney_Arts_Theatre "Putney Arts Theatre"), London in 2012.

### 2004–2010: The Dangermen and *The Liberty of Norton Folgate*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2004–2010: The Dangermen and The Liberty of Norton Folgate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Madness_at_Bimbos.jpg/250px-Madness_at_Bimbos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madness_at_Bimbos.jpg)

Madness performing live at Bimbos in 2005

In 2004, the band played a series of low-key concerts as the Dangermen, performing covers of classic reggae and ska songs. A lot of the songs were those played by the band when they were first forming,[[69]](#cite_note-suggsbbc04-70) and the band performed the songs as a celebration of their 25th anniversary.[[69]](#cite_note-suggsbbc04-70)

This led to the release of the cover album *[The Dangermen Sessions Vol. 1](/wiki/The_Dangermen_Sessions_Vol._1 "The Dangermen Sessions Vol. 1")* in August 2005 by [V2 Records](/wiki/V2_Records "V2 Records"). During the sessions which produced the album, in mid-2005, guitarist Chris Foreman announced his departure, citing "the petty, time consuming bollocks that goes on in the band" as his reason for leaving.[[70]](#cite_note-foremandeparture-71) The band completed the album without him, and on release, it peaked at no. 11 in the UK Albums Chart, which was the band's highest studio album chart position in 21 years. Although two singles were released, neither was a major success in the UK. The more successful of the two, "[Shame & Scandal](/wiki/Shame_%26_Scandal "Shame & Scandal")", reached number 38, but was more successful in France where it peaked at number 12.[[71]](#cite_note-shamecharts-72) "[Girl Why Don't You](/wiki/Girl_Why_Don%27t_You "Girl Why Don't You")" did not chart and the band left the V2 record label shortly after. At this time, Kevin Burdette joined as the band's guitarist for live appearances and also appeared in the videos for both "Sorry" and "NW5" in early 2007.

The six remaining original members of Madness began working on their first original studio album in seven years. In March 2007, the non-LP single ["Sorry"](/wiki/Sorry_(Madness_song) "Sorry (Madness song)") was released on the band's own record label [Lucky 7 Records](/wiki/Lucky_7_Records "Lucky 7 Records"), peaking in the UK charts at number 23. The single included a version featuring UK [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop "Hip hop") artists [Sway DaSafo](/wiki/Sway_DaSafo "Sway DaSafo") and [Baby Blue](/wiki/Baby_Blue_(rapper) "Baby Blue (rapper)").

The new Madness song "[NW5](/wiki/NW5 "NW5")" (then still titled "NW5 (I Would Give You Everything)") and a re-recorded version of "It Must Be Love" were featured in the German film *[Neues vom Wixxer](/w/index.php?title=Neues_vom_Wixxer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Neues vom Wixxer (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neues_vom_Wixxer "de:Neues vom Wixxer")]*. The two songs were released in Germany as a [double A-side](/wiki/Double_A-side "Double A-side"), and both of them were turned into music videos, which – besides members of the film's cast – featured Suggs, Chas Smash, Woody and stand-in guitarist Burdette. A re-recorded version of "NW5" was released as a single on 14 January 2008 in the UK, reaching no. 24 – this recording featured original Madness guitarist Chris Foreman, who had rejoined the band in time for the 2006 Christmas tour but had not participated in the original recording of the song.

In June 2008, Madness played the majority of their new studio album *[The Liberty of Norton Folgate](/wiki/The_Liberty_of_Norton_Folgate "The Liberty of Norton Folgate")* at London's [Hackney Empire](/wiki/Hackney_Empire "Hackney Empire") for three nights. The Hackney Empire performances were recorded and sold to fans on [USB](/wiki/USB "USB") wristbands as they left the show. Madness played two dates in December 2008, firstly in Manchester on 18 December, and secondly a return gig to The O2 in London on the 19th.

In December 2008, the band also announced that for their thirtieth anniversary in 2009, they would be staging a fifth Madstock Festival in London's Victoria Park on 17 July, 11 years after the last Madstock concert. It was originally rumoured that the newly reformed [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials") would make an appearance after finishing their reunion tour. However, this did not occur, although original Specials keyboardist Jerry Dammers – who was not part of the reunion line-up – was announced as a support act with [the Spatial AKA Orchestra](/wiki/The_Spatial_AKA_Orchestra "The Spatial AKA Orchestra") shortly before the festival. Dammers supported Madness again during their 2009 Christmas tour when he opened each night with a DJ set.

Through late March and early April 2009, the band played a series of festivals and separate headlining dates across Australia. The lead-up single from their latest studio album, titled ["Dust Devil](/wiki/Dust_Devil_(song) "Dust Devil (song)")", was released on 11 May on Lucky 7 Records. Actors [Alfie Allen](/wiki/Alfie_Allen "Alfie Allen") and [Jaime Winstone](/wiki/Jaime_Winstone "Jaime Winstone") co-starred in the music video. The single charted at No. 64 on the UK Singles Chart and at No. 1 on the UK Independent charts on 17 May 2009.

The new studio album, entitled *[The Liberty of Norton Folgate](/wiki/The_Liberty_of_Norton_Folgate "The Liberty of Norton Folgate")*, was released a week later, on 18 May 2009. It charted at No. 5 in the UK Albums Chart. The band continued to play various festivals, including [Pinkpop](/wiki/Pinkpop "Pinkpop"), [Splendour](/wiki/Splendour_in_Nottingham "Splendour in Nottingham"), and [Glastonbury](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival").[[72]](#cite_note-73) On 27 September 2009, the band also played a free concert on a closed-off [Regent Street](/wiki/Regent_Street "Regent Street") in association with [Absolute Radio](/wiki/Absolute_Radio "Absolute Radio").

As in previous years, the band embarked on a Christmas tour of the UK (also playing one concert in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin")), playing at various medium-sized venues. Mark Bedford took a break from the band and was replaced by Graham Bush for the tour.

Some members of the band appeared in [Catherine Tate](/wiki/Catherine_Tate "Catherine Tate")'s *Nan's Christmas Carol*. They first posed as carol singers, then played "Baggy Trousers" over the credits. On 18 January 2010, Madness released a fourth single, "[Forever Young](/wiki/Forever_Young_(Madness_song) "Forever Young (Madness song)")", from *The Liberty of Norton Folgate*. The single failed to chart.

During an interview with [RTÉ 2fm](/wiki/RT%C3%89_2fm "RTÉ 2fm") radio host [Dave Fanning](/wiki/Dave_Fanning "Dave Fanning") on 24 May 2010, drummer [Daniel Woodgate](/wiki/Daniel_Woodgate "Daniel Woodgate") stated that the members of the band were in the final stages of preparing songs for the follow-up to *The Liberty of Norton Folgate*. The band hoped to be able to start recording the album later in 2010.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

In September 2010, Madness were awarded the Idol Award at the [Q Awards](/wiki/Q_Awards "Q Awards") in London. Guitarist Chris Foreman stated in his acceptance speech that Madness were recording a new album.

Madness toured the UK throughout November and December 2010 with their final show at London's [Earl's Court](/wiki/Earl%27s_Court "Earl's Court"), where they played a new song from their upcoming album. However, two concerts, in [Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull") and [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield"), were cancelled due to heavy snowfall, although they were later rescheduled for 5 and 6 February 2011 respectively.

### 2011–2015: *Oui Oui, Si Si, Ja Ja, Da Da*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2011–2015: Oui Oui, Si Si, Ja Ja, Da Da")]

In June 2011, the band performed at [Meltdown](/wiki/Meltdown_(festival) "Meltdown (festival)") Festival at the [Royal Festival Hall](/wiki/Royal_Festival_Hall "Royal Festival Hall"), curated by inspiration [Ray Davies](/wiki/Ray_Davies "Ray Davies") of [the Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks"), who also introduced the band on stage. The concert served as the premiere of three new songs – "1978", "Can't Keep a Good Thing Down" and "Death of a Rude Boy".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Madness_perform_at_the_Olympic_Closing_Ceremony_%287890831596%29.jpg/250px-Madness_perform_at_the_Olympic_Closing_Ceremony_%287890831596%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madness_perform_at_the_Olympic_Closing_Ceremony_(7890831596).jpg)

Madness performing at the [2012 Summer Olympics closing ceremony](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics_closing_ceremony "2012 Summer Olympics closing ceremony") in London

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Madness_also_in_2014.JPG/250px-Madness_also_in_2014.JPG)](/wiki/File:Madness_also_in_2014.JPG)

Madness on stage at the [Manchester Arena](/wiki/Manchester_Arena "Manchester Arena") in 2014

The summer of 2012 saw two notable performances. In June, the band performed at the [Queen's Diamond Jubilee](/wiki/Diamond_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Diamond Jubilee of Elizabeth II") concert at [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace"). The band performed "Our House" and "It Must Be Love" from the roof of the palace with accompanying animations projected onto the palace front. In August, the band was the first to perform at the [closing ceremony](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics_closing_ceremony "2012 Summer Olympics closing ceremony") of the [London Olympic Games](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"). Departed bassist Bedford rejoined the band for both performances.[[74]](#cite_note-75) In August 2012, Madness released "Death of a Rude Boy" as a free teaser track from their new studio album. Madness' tenth studio album *[Oui Oui, Si Si, Ja Ja, Da Da](/wiki/Oui_Oui,_Si_Si,_Ja_Ja,_Da_Da "Oui Oui, Si Si, Ja Ja, Da Da")* was released on 29 October 2012 and entered the UK Albums Chart at no.10.[[75]](#cite_note-telegraph20120912-76) In January 2013 the album re-entered the chart at no. 16 on the back of the airplay success of the single "Never Knew Your Name".

From the start of 2013, Bedford increased his performances with the band, building to his full-time return to the group, which meant a close to the four years Graham Bush had spent with the band.[[76]](#cite_note-linkedin.com-77)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

On 22 March 2013, the band performed outside the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") [Television Centre](/wiki/Television_Centre,_London "Television Centre, London") in a live broadcast for [BBC Four](/wiki/BBC_Four "BBC Four"). This was followed by *Goodbye Television Centre*, a celebration of 50 years of the television centre, marking the closure of the building and the last show to be broadcast from it.[[77]](#cite_note-78) Following that, the band was the closing act to the new year celebration of 2014 in Dublin, Ireland.

On 22 March 2014, Suggs confirmed that Madness were writing a new studio album, which he stated "the band plan to record in the summer and release by the end of 2014".[[78]](#cite_note-79)

In October 2014, Cathal Smyth, aka Chas Smash, announced that he had left Madness to pursue a solo career. His debut solo studio album, *[A Comfortable Man](/wiki/A_Comfortable_Man "A Comfortable Man")*, was released on 11 May 2015 by Phoenix Rising Recording Co. Although Smyth's departure was characterised by him and the band at the time as a 'break' to concentrate on his solo career and not necessarily a permanent departure, Smyth has not rejoined Madness in over a decade since the break was announced. When asked about the matter in 2016, Suggs said, "This band is a very complicated entity. It’s a dysfunctional family, and within that you’re going to get dysfunctionality. But nothing is unresolvable, and the seven of us have always had tolerance and love for each other."[[79]](#cite_note-80)

### 2016–2022: *Can't Touch Us Now*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2016–2022: Can't Touch Us Now")]

The band announced their new studio album *[Can't Touch Us Now](/wiki/Can%27t_Touch_Us_Now "Can't Touch Us Now")* in May 2016. In support of the album, the band played the Pyramid Stage at the [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival") in June.[[80]](#cite_note-81) Lead single "Mr. Apples" was accompanied by a scripted video (first one since 2009) and A-listed by [BBC Radio 2](/wiki/BBC_Radio_2 "BBC Radio 2").[[81]](#cite_note-82) The song "Herbert" was released as a taster accompanied by an animation video.[[82]](#cite_note-83) Further singles "Can't Touch Us Now" and "Another Version of Me" were also playlisted by BBC Radio 2. The album itself entered the UK Albums Chart at number 5 in November 2016.[[83]](#cite_note-84) The band finished the year on a UK arena tour in December.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

Throughout 2017, the band played at several festivals and concerts worldwide, including mainland Europe, Asia, and Australia. In April, their first Australian gig at the Fremantle Arts Centre in Western Australia sold out, necessitating a second gig the following night. In August, the band hosted their own "House of Common Festival" for the second year on [Clapham Common](/wiki/Clapham_Common "Clapham Common"). This was the band's only London gig of the year.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

In 2018, the band embarked on a summer tour of stately homes in the UK before a Christmas arena tour in December.[[86]](#cite_note-87)[[87]](#cite_note-88) Madness performed on New Year's Eve through to New Year's Day at "Madness Rocks Big Ben Live". The entire performance was shown on [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One") before and after that year's fireworks.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

In March 2019, Madness announced the release of their group autobiography, *Before We Was We: Madness by Madness*, to be released on hardback that October.[[89]](#cite_note-90) The book includes commentary from all seven members telling the story of their early days and childhoods up until their breakthrough as a group. In May 2021, the book was adapted as a three-part TV documentary on [AMC](/wiki/AMC_(European_TV_channel) "AMC (European TV channel)"), with the first part free on [BT](/wiki/BT_Group "BT Group")'s [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") channel.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

The band celebrated 40 years since their debut studio album release with several special live appearances throughout 2019. The band performed at their own "House of Common" festival in August and held a special concert with a full orchestra at [Kenwood House](/wiki/Kenwood_House "Kenwood House"). In November, they played at [Electric Ballroom](/wiki/Electric_Ballroom "Electric Ballroom") in Camden, forty years to the day since one of their first appearances there. The performance was broadcast on [Sky Arts](/wiki/Sky_Arts "Sky Arts") in 2020. The band saw the year out with three concerts at [The Roundhouse](/wiki/The_Roundhouse "The Roundhouse").[[91]](#cite_note-92)

In December 2019, the band released a new single, "Bullingdon Boys (Don't Get Bullied by the Bully Boys)". The *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* described the song as a 'barbed swipe at [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") and his [Eton](/wiki/Eton_College "Eton College") cohorts'.[[92]](#cite_note-93) In March 2020, Madness were honoured on the Camden 'Music Walk Of Fame' with a stone unveiling, which saw members [Suggs](/wiki/Suggs "Suggs"), [Mike Barson](/wiki/Mike_Barson "Mike Barson"), [Mark Bedford](/wiki/Mark_Bedford "Mark Bedford"), [Dan Woodgate](/wiki/Dan_Woodgate "Dan Woodgate"), as well as former member [Cathal Smyth](/wiki/Chas_Smash "Chas Smash"), in attendance.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

In April 2021, following some special videos created during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), Madness announced their first global live stream, titled "The Get Up!", to be held the next month. It featured live music and comedy from the group and [Charlie Higson](/wiki/Charlie_Higson "Charlie Higson"), pre-recorded at the [London Palladium](/wiki/London_Palladium "London Palladium").[[94]](#cite_note-95) During the live stream, the band was accompanied by [Roland Gift](/wiki/Roland_Gift "Roland Gift") of [Fine Young Cannibals](/wiki/Fine_Young_Cannibals "Fine Young Cannibals") and [Paul Weller](/wiki/Paul_Weller "Paul Weller") of [the Jam](/wiki/The_Jam "The Jam") and had a cameo by [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II"), played by Mike Barson.[[95]](#cite_note-96) They announced their 2021 tour, The Ladykillers Tour, the next day, to be held alongside [Squeeze](/wiki/Squeeze_(band) "Squeeze (band)").[[96]](#cite_note-97)

### 2023–present: *Theatre of the Absurd Presents C'est la Vie*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2023–present: Theatre of the Absurd Presents C'est la Vie")]

In September 2023, the band announced their thirteenth studio album *[Theatre of the Absurd Presents C'est la Vie](/wiki/Theatre_of_the_Absurd_Presents_C%27est_la_Vie "Theatre of the Absurd Presents C'est la Vie")*, which was released on 17 November 2023. It was followed by a UK tour with the [Lightning Seeds](/wiki/Lightning_Seeds "Lightning Seeds"), including stops in Liverpool, Manchester and London.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Graham_%22Suggs%22_McPherson.jpg/250px-Graham_%22Suggs%22_McPherson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Graham_%22Suggs%22_McPherson.jpg)

Graham "Suggs" McPherson performs with Madness at MGM Fenway in Boston on the Theatre of the Absurd Presents C'est la Vie tour on 29 May 2024

The album was preceded by the single "C'est La Vie" and "Baby Burglar", a song inspired by the antics of band members in their youth, including breaking into singer-songwriter [Lynsey de Paul](/wiki/Lynsey_de_Paul "Lynsey de Paul")'s house.[[98]](#cite_note-99) A new video featured these two singles plus a performance of *Round We Go*.

It would become Madness' first ever number one studio album in the UK.[[99]](#cite_note-100) The album also made the album charts in Ireland, Germany[[100]](#cite_note-101) and The Netherlands.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

On 11 December, a full version of the "C'est La Vie" video was released, followed by "Baby Burglar" on 23 January 2024.

In February, it was announced that "Round We Go" would be released as the official new single. A full video was released on 6 February, followed by a single edit on 16 February. On 23 February the song was added to BBC Radio 2's New Music Playlist.[[102]](#cite_note-BBC_–_Radio_2_New_Music_Playlist-103)

In April, the enhanced version of *Theatre Of The Absurd Presents C'est La Vie* was announced, featuring 5 new songs and 7 unreleased live songs from the December tour, including the band's version of The Specials' "Friday Night Saturday Morning". "No Reason" – written by Suggs – was made available directly.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

On 17 May – just before the band would go on their first US tour in 12 years – "Hour Of Need" was released as a single and went straight to the BBC Radio 2 playlist.[[102]](#cite_note-BBC_–_Radio_2_New_Music_Playlist-103) The release was accompanied by the announcement of the 'Hour Of Need' Award, for which fans could honour an unsung hero in their lives.[[104]](#cite_note-105)

Recently, in August 2025, guitarist [Chris Foreman](/wiki/Chris_Foreman "Chris Foreman") revealed that he had been diagnosed with [myeloma](/wiki/Myeloma "Myeloma"), a treatable but incurable form of [blood cancer](/wiki/Tumors_of_the_hematopoietic_and_lymphoid_tissues "Tumors of the hematopoietic and lymphoid tissues"). He first experienced symptoms earlier that year, including severe pain in his upper back and shoulders, which led to an [MRI scan](/wiki/MRI_scan "MRI scan") and the discovery of a [spinal tumour](/wiki/Spinal_tumor "Spinal tumor"). Following [radiotherapy](/wiki/Radiation_therapy "Radiation therapy") and a [spinal tap](/wiki/Spinal_tap_(medical_procedure) "Spinal tap (medical procedure)") procedure, Foreman stated that his [kidney function](/wiki/Assessment_of_kidney_function "Assessment of kidney function")—initially at 14%—had "greatly improved." He stated that despite recent recovery, he would not be able to participate in the band's upcoming tours for the rest of the year, but expressed hope of achieving remission and returning to performing in 2026.[[105]](#cite_note-106)

### The Madness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: The Madness")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/fb/The_Madness_Band.jpg/250px-The_Madness_Band.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Madness_Band.jpg)

A promotional photograph of The Madness (left to right): Thompson, Foreman, Smash, and Suggs

The Madness was a line-up of Madness without bassist Mark Bedford, drummer Daniel Woodgate and keyboardist Mike Barson, active between 1987 and 1988. Formed by Suggs, Chas Smash, Lee Thompson and Chris Foreman in early 1987, they distinguished this line-up from the previous Madness line-up only by adding the word "The" to the band's name. The band had held a competition to find a new name on BBC Radio 1. They decided on 'The Wasp Factory' after the [Iain Banks](/wiki/Iain_Banks "Iain Banks") novel of [the same name](/wiki/The_Wasp_Factory "The Wasp Factory"), but that name had already been taken by another band.

The album is notable for featuring guest performers including the Specials' [Jerry Dammers](/wiki/Jerry_Dammers "Jerry Dammers"), early Madness member John Hasler and Earl Falconer of [UB40](/wiki/UB40 "UB40"). It is also the first studio album produced by the band themselves under the alias 'The Three Eyes'. When they first regrouped in early 1987, they also went to meet Barson in Amsterdam to ask if he wanted to come back, but he refused.

*The Guinness Book of British Hit Singles* and many online discographies consider this band to be the same as "Madness". Several of the songs on the album ("4BF", "Be Good Boy", "Gabriels Horn", "In Wonder") were reworked songs from the cancelled 1986 Madness studio album and had been performed live or demoed in 1986 before the band broke up. Lee Thompson and Chris Foreman also alluded to this view on their sole studio album *Crunch!* (1990), which was dedicated to "the good ship Madness and all who sailed in her (1979 to 1989)".

## Collaborations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Collaborations")]

Madness collaborated with [Elvis Costello](/wiki/Elvis_Costello "Elvis Costello") in 1983 on a version of their song "[Tomorrow's (Just Another Day)](/wiki/Tomorrow%27s_(Just_Another_Day) "Tomorrow's (Just Another Day)")". It was released as a bonus track to the 12" copy of the single. In later years, Barson stated that Costello's "[Watching the Detectives](/wiki/Watching_the_Detectives_(song) "Watching the Detectives (song)")" was the main influence on the song "My Girl". They were also asked to participate in [Live Aid](/wiki/Live_Aid "Live Aid"), but declined as they were in a period of turmoil at the time and were very busy rehearsing songs for *Mad Not Mad*. However, Suggs, Carl, and Lee did participate in [Fashion Aid](/wiki/Fashion_Aid "Fashion Aid") instead.

Several members of the band also collaborated with [Morrissey](/wiki/Morrissey "Morrissey") in his early solo career. Having been a Madness fan himself, Morrissey started working with former Madness producers [Clive Langer](/wiki/Clive_Langer "Clive Langer") and [Alan Winstanley](/wiki/Alan_Winstanley "Alan Winstanley") in 1989. Morrissey then recruited [Suggs](/wiki/Suggs "Suggs") in December of 1989 to help him complete a new album, which was scrapped and saw the compilation album *[Bona Drag](/wiki/Bona_Drag "Bona Drag")* released instead in October 1990.[[110]](#cite_note-111) Suggs contributed backing vocals on the single "[Piccadilly Palare](/wiki/Piccadilly_Palare "Piccadilly Palare")" and it's B-side "Get Off The Stage". Morrissey also befriended Cathal during recording sessions for *[Kill Uncle](/wiki/Kill_Uncle "Kill Uncle")* in late 1990, who introduced him to [Boz Boorer](/wiki/Boz_Boorer "Boz Boorer"), who continued to work with Morrissey for the next 3 decades. Morrissey tried to recruit Cathal as his manager, but instead had him contribute backing vocals to his cover of "That's Entertainment", the B-side to his single "[Sing Your Life](/wiki/Sing_Your_Life "Sing Your Life")", which also had Suggs return on backing vocals and Mark Bedford on bass, who had played bass on the entirety of the *Kill Uncle* album. [[111]](#cite_note-112)

For *Wonderful* in 1999, [Ian Dury](/wiki/Ian_Dury "Ian Dury") laid down vocals on the track "[Drip Fed Fred](/wiki/Drip_Fed_Fred "Drip Fed Fred")" which was released as the last single from the album. It was Dury's last recording before his death. Ill health prevented Dury from actively promoting the single, although he did appear on the [National Lottery Show](/wiki/National_Lottery_Show "National Lottery Show"). For a later performance on *[TFI Friday](/wiki/TFI_Friday "TFI Friday")*, the song was reworked to incorporate [Phill Jupitus](/wiki/Phill_Jupitus "Phill Jupitus") on vocals. Live, Madness have collaborated with artists including [UB40](/wiki/UB40 "UB40") and [Prince Buster](/wiki/Prince_Buster "Prince Buster"), notably at their first Madstock concert. They have also played live frequently with members of the other 2 Tone bands, including the Specials. In May 2008, Suggs and Carl performed live with [Pet Shop Boys](/wiki/Pet_Shop_Boys "Pet Shop Boys") at London's [Heaven](/wiki/Heaven_(nightclub) "Heaven (nightclub)") collaborating on a new arrangement of "My Girl", as part of a tribute evening to their former minder [Dainton 'The Bear' Connell](/wiki/Dainton_Connell "Dainton Connell"), called *Can You Bear It?*.[[112]](#cite_note-113)[[113]](#cite_note-Pet_News-114)[[114]](#cite_note-115) A few days afterwards, [Pet Shop Boys](/wiki/Pet_Shop_Boys "Pet Shop Boys") posted their own version of the track on their official website.

In late 2010, the band collaborated in the *Cage Against the Machine* project, in which numerous artists performed [John Cage](/wiki/John_Cage "John Cage")'s *[4′33″](/wiki/4%E2%80%B233%E2%80%B3 "4′33″")* for a [charity single](/wiki/Charity_single "Charity single") intended to prevent the winner of [*The X Factor*](/wiki/The_X_Factor_(British_TV_series) "The X Factor (British TV series)") claiming the Christmas Number 1. The title refers to the previous year's successful campaign to get [Rage Against the Machine](/wiki/Rage_Against_the_Machine "Rage Against the Machine")'s "[Killing in the Name](/wiki/Killing_in_the_Name "Killing in the Name")" to chart above *X Factor* winner [Joe McElderry](/wiki/Joe_McElderry "Joe McElderry").

## Lyrical themes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Lyrical themes")]

Frequent themes in Madness' songs included childhood memories (e.g., "[Baggy Trousers](/wiki/Baggy_Trousers "Baggy Trousers")", and "[Our House](/wiki/Our_House_(Madness_song) "Our House (Madness song)")") and petty crime (e.g., "[Shut Up](/wiki/Shut_Up_(Madness_song) "Shut Up (Madness song)")", and "Deceives the Eye"). Although Madness were seen by some as somewhat of a humorous band with catchy, bouncy songs,[[115]](#cite_note-absolutelyam-116) many of their songs took a darker tone (such as the singles "[Grey Day](/wiki/Grey_Day "Grey Day")" and "[Tomorrow's (Just Another Day)](/wiki/Tomorrow%27s_(Just_Another_Day) "Tomorrow's (Just Another Day)")") and they sometimes tackled what were, at the time, controversial issues in their lyrics. "Embarrassment" (from the *Absolutely* album) was written by Lee Thompson, and reflected the unfolding turmoil following the news that his teenage sister had become pregnant and was carrying a black man's child.[[116]](#cite_note-embarrassment-117) Madness discussed [animal testing](/wiki/Animal_testing "Animal testing") in the song "Tomorrow's Dream".[[117]](#cite_note-animaltesting-118) The band criticised the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service") (NHS) in "Mrs. Hutchinson", which told the story of a woman who, after several misdiagnoses and mistreatment, became terminally ill. The story was based on the experiences of Mike Barson's mother.[[118]](#cite_note-mrsh-119) Madness' final single prior to disbanding, "[(Waiting For) The Ghost Train](/wiki/(Waiting_For)_The_Ghost_Train "(Waiting For) The Ghost Train")", commented on [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[38]](#cite_note-madness86-39)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madness_(band)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Awards")]

The band's first notable musical award came in 1983 when Chris Foreman and Cathal Smyth won an [Ivor Novello Award](/wiki/Ivor_Novello_Award "Ivor Novello Award") for *Best Song* for the international hit "Our House".[[3]](#cite_note-suggsimdb-4)[[27]](#cite_note-madness83-28) Madness received another Ivor Novello Award 17 years later for an "Outstanding Song Collection".[[3]](#cite_note-suggsimdb-4)[[5]](#cite_note-novellobbc-6) In 2005, they were awarded the [Mojo](/wiki/Mojo_Awards "Mojo Awards") "Hall of Fame" Award, notably for being "an artist's artist".[[119]](#cite_note-mojoaward-120) In 2007, a campaign took place by fans of Madness for the band to be awarded a [Brit Award](/wiki/Brit_Award "Brit Award").[[120]](#cite_note-britaward-121) Many fans and critics feel they have been overlooked over their past 30 years in the music industry.[[120]](#cite_note-britaward-121) In July 2009, Madness were awarded the 'Silver Clef' Icon Award. In September 2010, Madness were awarded the 'Idol Award' at the 2010 [Q Awards](/wiki/Q_Awards "Q Awards") in London.
